title: Banning Russian soldiers from the EU is a common sense security measure
gist: Elena Davlikanova and Tatiana Vorozhko argue the EU's proposed visa ban on anyone who served in the Russian Armed Forces since 2022 — part of von der Leyen's 21st sanctions package, but currently blocked by French and Italian resistance — is a proportionate hybrid-warfare countermeasure given roughly one million current combatants, documented war-crimes patterns, and Russia's already-active sabotage campaigns inside Europe.

insights.claim: European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen proposed on June 9, 2026 banning entry into the EU for anyone who has served in the Russian Armed Forces since the war began, as part of the bloc's twenty-first sanctions package.
insights.speaker: Elena Davlikanova, Tatiana Vorozhko
insights.so_what: A concrete near-term EU policy catalyst to track; adoption would materially tighten Russian nationals' mobility into the Schengen area.
insights.quote: For the first time, we propose to ban from entry into the EU anyone who has served in the Russian Armed Forces since the beginning of the war. Europe stays off limits for anyone who has participated in the invasion of Ukraine. As simple as that,

insights.claim: The ban requires unanimous approval from all EU capitals, and France and Italy — which receive the highest number of Russian visa applications among EU countries — are currently resisting it on legal and technical grounds.
insights.speaker: Elena Davlikanova, Tatiana Vorozhko
insights.so_what: Identifies the two specific veto-risk states; France and Italy's stance is the key variable determining whether the sanctions package passes.
insights.quote: France and Italy currently receive the highest number of visa applications from Russia among EU countries.
insights.locator: Recent reporting indicates that both Paris and Rome

insights.claim: The visa-ban idea originated in spring 2026 from eight EU member states (Germany, Sweden, Finland, Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, Romania, and Poland), who characterized unrestricted entry for Russian military personnel as one of the most serious internal security risks arising from the war.
insights.speaker: Elena Davlikanova, Tatiana Vorozhko
insights.so_what: Names the coalition of frontline/northern states pushing the policy, useful for tracking which EU blocs align on future Russia-facing security measures.
insights.quote: one of the most serious risks to the EU’s internal security arising from the war in Ukraine

insights.claim: EU visa policy toward Russian nationals has so far been inconsistent, limited to individual countries, creating loopholes that let Russian passport holders travel throughout the Schengen area via visas issued by any single member state.
insights.speaker: Elena Davlikanova, Tatiana Vorozhko
insights.so_what: Explains why a bloc-wide rule (rather than country-by-country restrictions) is the authors' preferred fix — a patchwork policy is functionally porous.
insights.quote: Russian passport holders in many cases able to travel throughout the EU via Schengen visas
insights.locator: This inconsistency has created numerous loopholes

insights.claim: Convicted criminals have reportedly been recruited into the Russian military since 2022 in exchange for reduced sentences, raising the risk profile of veterans who could later seek EU entry.
insights.speaker: Elena Davlikanova, Tatiana Vorozhko
insights.so_what: A specific demographic risk factor cited to justify why a blanket veteran ban, not individualized screening, is proposed as the tool.
insights.quote: the large number of convicted criminals who have reportedly been recruited into the Russian military since 2022 to join the invasion of Ukraine in exchange for reduced sentences
insights.locator: It also noted the large number of convicted criminals

insights.claim: Estonian Foreign Minister Margus Tsahkna said Russia currently has close to one million combatants and warned that hundreds of thousands of ex-combatants could seek to enter Europe once the war ends, even as Russia is already using other operatives to attack European societies.
insights.speaker: Elena Davlikanova, Tatiana Vorozhko
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of the future mobility risk (hundreds of thousands of potential entrants), the core number behind the urgency argument for a pre-emptive ban.
insights.quote: We have close to one million combatants in Russia right now,
insights.locator: Advocates of a visa ban say

insights.claim: The Reckoning Project, a global team of journalists and lawyers, has collected over 800 testimonies since 2022 documenting systematic criminal behavior by Russian military personnel on occupied Ukrainian territory outside of combat activities.
insights.speaker: Elena Davlikanova, Tatiana Vorozhko
insights.so_what: Gives an evidentiary base size for atrocity documentation, relevant to future war-crimes prosecutions and to the argument that Russian veterans as a class carry elevated risk.
insights.quote: over 800 testimonies collected since 2022 by The Reckoning Project

insights.claim: Recent revelations of Russian ties to arson attacks on properties linked to UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer illustrate that Kremlin hybrid-war operations — including sabotage, cyberattacks, and disinformation — already extend across Europe, not just Ukraine.
insights.speaker: Elena Davlikanova, Tatiana Vorozhko
insights.so_what: A concrete, high-profile UK incident used as evidence that Russian hybrid warfare against Europe is active now, strengthening the case for pre-emptive mobility restrictions.
insights.quote: Recent details of Russian ties to arson attacks on properties linked to British Prime Minister Keir Starmer have underlined the scope of the Kremlin’s hybrid warfare operations across Europe.
insights.locator: Recent details of Russian ties to arson attacks

insights.claim: Opponents argue the visa ban amounts to collective punishment that is legally questionable and could further isolate ordinary Russians at a time when engagement might be more strategically useful.
insights.speaker: Elena Davlikanova, Tatiana Vorozhko
insights.so_what: Represents the case against the policy fairly, which is the counterweight investors should note when assessing the probability the ban actually clears unanimous approval.
insights.quote: limiting access to EU visas would represent a form of collective punishment that is legally questionable and contradicts European values

insights.claim: Putin's rhetoric of reclaiming 'historically Russian lands' would, at the Russian Empire's greatest historical extent, cover territory belonging to five current EU member states, framing unrestricted Russian military mobility as a long-run security risk even beyond the current war.
insights.speaker: Elena Davlikanova, Tatiana Vorozhko
insights.so_what: Extends the risk horizon beyond the current war, arguing the security rationale for restricting Russian military personnel persists even after a ceasefire or peace deal.
insights.quote: insisting he is reclaiming “historically Russian lands.”

insights.claim: Even proponents concede a ban on Russian military personnel would be technically difficult to implement and would not itself stop Moscow's hybrid warfare, but the authors argue inaction sends a message that Europe is divided and lacks political will on security.
insights.speaker: Elena Davlikanova, Tatiana Vorozhko
insights.so_what: A candid limits-of-the-policy admission paired with a symbolic-deterrence argument — useful for calibrating how much actual security benefit versus signaling value the measure would deliver.
insights.quote: Any attempt to ban Russian military personnel from the EU would be technically difficult to implement and would not prevent Moscow from continuing to wage hybrid warfare across Europe.
insights.locator: Any attempt to ban Russian military personnel
